title: How to Talk to Your Child About a School Shooting Drill Without Increasing Anxiety
author: Tklesson Team
content:
Your child comes home from school and mentions, casually or anxiously, that they practiced hiding from a bad person today. They describe crouching in the dark, staying silent, waiting for an all-clear signal. They may have questions. They may seem unfazed. They may have nightmares. Lockdown drills are now a routine part of the school experience for most American children, but the conversation about them at home is often rushed, avoided, or handled in ways that inadvertently increase fear. Your child needs to understand these drills without being traumatized by them. Here is how to have that conversation in an age-appropriate way.

Why the Conversation Matters

Children fill in the gaps with their own imaginations, and their imaginations are often more frightening than reality. A child who is not given a clear explanation of why the drill happened and what it means may construct their own narrative. The narrative may involve imminent danger, constant threat, or a world where bad people are always trying to hurt them. The silence from adults communicates that the topic is too scary to discuss, which makes it scarier. The child who cannot talk about their fear carries it alone.

The conversation also provides an opportunity to reinforce your child's sense of safety. The underlying message of any discussion about school safety drills should be: the adults in your life are working hard to keep you safe. The drill is one of the ways they do that. The message shifts the focus from the threat to the protection. The child learns that bad things can happen, and that there are systems in place to respond to them.

Avoiding the conversation does not protect your child from anxiety. It leaves them alone with their anxiety. A calm, honest, age-appropriate conversation reduces anxiety by providing a framework for understanding what happened. The child who can make sense of the drill is less frightened by it than the child who experiences it as a confusing and inexplicable disruption.

Age-Appropriate Scripts

For preschoolers and early elementary students: Keep it simple and concrete. You can say: Sometimes schools practice what to do if there is an emergency, just like we practice fire drills. The practice helps everyone know what to do to stay safe. The adults at your school are trained to keep you safe, and that is why you practice. Focus on the safety message rather than the nature of the threat. Very young children do not need to know what specific danger the drill is preparing for. The analogy to fire drills is helpful because fire drills are familiar and generally not frightening to children. The lockdown drill is another kind of safety practice, nothing more.

For upper elementary students: These children are more aware of the world and may have heard about school shootings from peers, media, or overheard adult conversations. You can say: You may have heard that sometimes bad people try to hurt others at schools. This is very rare, but schools practice how to stay safe just in case. The drill you did is one of those practices. It helps everyone know exactly what to do so they can stay safe. Do you have any questions about it? The acknowledgment that the threat exists is honest. The emphasis on rarity and preparation is reassuring. The invitation to ask questions keeps the conversation open.

For middle school students and older: These students can handle more complexity. You can say: School shootings are extremely rare, but they are serious, and schools have a responsibility to be prepared. The lockdown drills are one part of that preparation. I know they can feel scary or strange. How did you feel during the drill? The conversation can include a discussion of the statistical rarity of school shootings and the broader context of school safety. It can also include a discussion of advocacy, if your child expresses a desire to be involved in efforts to reduce gun violence or improve school safety. The goal is not to impose a political perspective but to help your child process their feelings and, if they wish, channel them into action.

What to Avoid

Do not make promises you cannot keep. Telling your child that nothing bad will ever happen at their school is reassuring in the moment but undermines trust when they encounter a drill or hear about an event elsewhere. Say instead: The adults at your school work very hard to keep you safe every day.

Do not share graphic details about school shootings, either from the news or from your own anxieties. Your child does not need to know the specifics of past events to understand the purpose of the drill. The details increase fear without increasing understanding.

Do not dismiss your child's fear. Telling a child that they are overreacting or that the drill is no big deal communicates that their emotions are unacceptable. Acknowledge the feeling: It makes sense that the drill felt scary. A lot of kids feel that way. Would you like to talk about it? The acknowledgment validates the emotion without amplifying it.

Monitor your own anxiety. Children take emotional cues from their parents. If you are visibly distressed about the drill, your child will absorb your distress. Process your own feelings with another adult, not with your child. The conversation with your child should be calm, steady, and reassuring, even if you are managing your own complex emotions about the topic.

The Bottom Line

School safety drills are a reality of modern education. Your child needs to understand them without being traumatized by them. Tailor the conversation to your child's age. Emphasize that the drill is a safety practice, like a fire drill. Acknowledge feelings without dismissing them. Avoid graphic details and false promises. The goal is not to eliminate your child's awareness of danger. It is to help them understand that the adults around them are prepared and working to keep them safe.
